Skip to main content
Mobius
Loop How it works CLI github.com/AaronGoldsmith/mobius
$ pip install -e ".[dev]" Python 3.12+ · MIT
Adversarial Agent Swarm Orchestrator

Agents compete.
Losers evolve.
The best survive.

Möbius runs Claude, Gemini, and GPT agents against each other on real tasks. A cross-family judge panel eliminates self-preference bias. Elo tracks who's actually winning. The swarm improves itself.

01 — The competition loop

How the swarm runs
loop running
Swarm models Haiku · Flash · 4o-mini (cheap, parallel)
Judge models Opus · Gemini Pro · GPT-4o (cross-family)
Evolution model Opus via Claude Code Pro (free)
Loop trigger Losers only — champions never touched

02 — Step by step

01
Assemble the swarm

Agents from Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI register in a shared SQLite registry. Each carries a system prompt and a capability set — not just text, but tools and retrieval patterns.

02
Run the competition

All registered agents receive the same task simultaneously. Haiku, Flash, and 4o-mini handle cheap parallel execution. Outputs are collected and anonymized before judging.

03
Cross-family judgment

Claude Opus, Gemini Pro, and GPT-4o score outputs independently. No single family holds veto power. Consensus across adversarial providers surfaces genuine quality.

04
Evolve the losers

Underperformers get targeted — not champions. Opus reads the winner's output against the loser's prompt, then rewrites the gap. Each iteration is logged and reversible.

03 — Core concepts

Elo Rating System

Every agent has an Elo score updated after each match based on win probability — the same system used in chess and competitive gaming. Over hundreds of rounds, the leaderboard reflects genuine, statistically grounded capability.

Cross-Family Bias Prevention

If Claude judges Claude, the deck is stacked. The three-judge panel always spans all three AI families. A consensus vote across providers produces verdicts no single model can game through self-preference.

Skills as Differentiator

Agents don't compete on prompts alone. Each carries a capability set — tools, memory access, retrieval patterns. Two agents with identical system prompts but different skills produce meaningfully different results.

Free Tier via Claude Code Pro

Opus-grade judgment and agent creation run as Claude Code skills — free on Pro subscription. Only cheap swarm execution hits the API. The most expensive cognitive layer costs nothing extra.

Vector Memory

SQLite with sqlite-vec stores agent embeddings and match history. Semantic search surfaces similar past tasks for context injection — no external vector database, no extra infrastructure.

Targeted Evolution

Evolution targets underperformers, not champions. Opus reads winner output and loser prompt side-by-side, then rewrites the gap. The loop runs until performance plateaus or a round limit is hit.

04 — Architecture

Execution layer
Swarm agents Claude Haiku · Gemini Flash · GPT-4o-mini
Judge panel Claude Opus · Gemini Pro · GPT-4o
Parallelism All agents run simultaneously per round
Evolution model Opus via Claude Code (free on Pro)
Cost profile Haiku/Flash for bulk · Opus-free for quality
Storage & memory
Runtime Python 3.12+
Registry SQLite — agents, matches, Elo history
Vectors sqlite-vec — embeddings, similarity search
SDKs claude-agent-sdk · openai · google-genai
Install pip install -e ".[dev]"

05 — CLI

bash — mobius
# one-time setup
$ pip install -e ".[dev]"
$ mobius init
$ mobius bootstrap
# seeds initial agent pool via API (~$1.50 one-time cost)
# run a competition round
$ mobius run "write a sorting algorithm in Python"
# all agents execute in parallel → judge panel scores → Elo updated
$ mobius leaderboard
# current Elo rankings across all agents
$ mobius explain
# show last match's judge reasoning verbatim
# self-improvement
$ mobius evolve
# Opus rewrites underperforming agents (free via Claude Code Pro)
$ mobius loop --rounds 10
# full self-improvement cycle: run → judge → evolve × N
# or use Claude Code skills — free on Pro
> /mobius-seed "code generation specialist"
# Opus creates agents directly in your conversation
> /mobius-judge
# Opus judges the latest match outputs
> /mobius-audit quick
# health check: database, agents, last competition results

06 — What this is

Benchmark leaderboards tell you how a model performs on their test. Möbius tells you how agents perform on your task — with your data, your tools, your definition of good.

The adversarial structure matters. When the judging model is also a competitor in the next round, bias surfaces and gets averaged out. No single provider holds veto power.

Evolution closes the gap between what a model can do and what your agents actually do. Each iteration is targeted, logged, and reversible. You end up with a team shaped by evidence, not intuition.

"The best agent for your task is the one that proved it — by beating the others."
Möbius
  • github.com/AaronGoldsmith/mobius
  • anthropic
  • openai
  • google-genai
